agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is being designed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory, non-negotiable requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, while Operations demands no location sharing to protect driver retention.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver fulfilling an order, whose location is captured via GPS during active deliveries."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The period from when a courier picks up a package to when it is delivered to the customer."},{"term":"Customer visibility","definition":"The degree to which a customer can see courier position or delivery progress."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which courier position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to approximate zone or ETA-only."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Courier location data must only be collected and used during an active delivery lifecycle (pickup to dropoff).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tracking a courier outside of an active delivery violates privacy and has no business justification. This is a data-integrity and privacy constraint.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that no courier GPS data is collected or stored when the courier has no active delivery assignment."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Customers must receive some form of delivery progress information during an active delivery.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The form of progress information (GPS, ETA, zone, status updates) is a policy decision, but providing zero information is a business choice, not a system-integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that a customer with an active delivery can retrieve at least one progress indicator (e.g., status, ETA, or location approximation)."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The level of courier location granularity exposed to customers must be explicitly defined by a product-level policy decision that balances customer transparency with courier privacy and retention.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy that could be tuned over time — exact coordinates, approximate zone, ETA-only, etc.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that the system's customer-facing location response respects a configurable granularity setting (e.g., EXACT, APPROXIMATE, ETA_ONLY, NONE)."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Courier privacy preferences or organizational privacy policy must be enforceable at the system level.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If the system cannot enforce a privacy policy, any decision made to resolve this conflict is unenforceable, making the system state inconsistent with stated policy.","testable_assertion":"ASSERT that the tracking API filters courier location data through a privacy/granularity policy before any customer-facing response is emitted."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer has an active delivery in progress","when":"The customer requests delivery tracking information","then":"The system returns progress information filtered through the configured visibility policy (granularity level), never raw GPS coordinates unless policy explicitly permits it."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"No active delivery exists for the customer","when":"The customer requests courier location","then":"The system returns no location data and an appropriate status message."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The visibility policy is set to APPROXIMATE","when":"The customer views tracking","then":"The system shows a fuzzy zone (e.g., street-level or neighborhood) and ETA, not exact coordinates."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"The visibility policy is set to ETA_ONLY","when":"The customer views tracking","then":"The system shows only estimated time of arrival with no map pin or coordinate data."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A courier is on an active delivery","when":"Location data is persisted","then":"The raw GPS coordinates are stored only in an internal-access data store, never directly in a customer-facing read model without policy filtering."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier GPS signal is lost or stale during active delivery","expected_behavior":"System should display last known status with a staleness indicator or fall back to ETA-based estimate; never show stale coordinates as if they are live."},{"scenario":"Customer screenshots or shares approximate location data externally","expected_behavior":"Approximate location (zone/ETA) limits the precision of leaked data, reducing courier surveillance risk. Exact GPS would make this a severe privacy exposure."},{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for an extended period (e.g., traffic, multi-stop)","expected_behavior":"System should avoid exposing exact stationary location which could enable in-person confrontation; approximate zone or status update ('courier is nearby') is safer."},{"scenario":"Delivery marked complete but customer still requests tracking","expected_behavior":"System returns delivery-complete status; no further location data is provided."},{"scenario":"Multiple concurrent deliveries by the same courier","expected_behavior":"Each customer sees only progress for their own delivery; no cross-delivery location leakage."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Driver churn from surveillance concerns directly increases recruitment and training costs (typically $3K-$5K per driver in logistics). Conversely, lack of tracking transparency may reduce customer conversion and increase 'where is my order' support contacts, each costing $5-$12. The resolution must optimize both retention economics.","operational":"Raw GPS exposure creates liability (driver safety, data privacy regulation). A configurable granularity policy adds moderate system complexity but enables future tuning without re-architecture.","user":"Customers expect delivery visibility — it is an industry standard (UPS, DoorDash, Uber). However, industry leaders use approximate tracking (moving car icon on a map, ETA), not raw lat/long. Customers are satisfied with approximate + ETA.","risk":"HIGH — shipping either team's non-negotiable requirement as-is creates existential risk. Exact GPS loses drivers. No visibility loses customers. Both paths lose revenue. Additionally, exact GPS may trigger GDPR/CCPA obligations around real-time worker surveillance. A privacy-by-design approach (approximate by default) mitigates regulatory risk.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Desire delivery visibility and progress tracking. Satisfied by approximate location + ETA in industry benchmarks. Exact GPS is not a proven customer need."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected by surveillance perception. Driver retention is operationally critical — a courier shortage halts the entire business."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Wants transparency as a differentiator. Needs to understand that approximate tracking IS transparency and is industry standard. Exact GPS is not required to achieve their goal."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Protecting driver retention. Their blanket 'never show location' stance overreacts — approximate tracking is the industry norm and does not cause the surveillance perception that exact GPS does."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Not yet consulted but must be — real-time GPS sharing to third parties (customers) may trigger data protection obligations depending on jurisdiction."},{"group":"Product Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. Neither team has the authority to set company-wide policy on location visibility unilaterally."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Two explicit, non-negotiable stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other. Neither can be implemented as stated without violating the other. The spec cannot proceed without executive arbitration. Additionally, legal/compliance input is absent, which could further constrain options.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unresolvable without escalation","Industry standard is approximate tracking, not exact GPS — this is well-established","A configurable granularity policy is the correct architectural approach regardless of resolution","Courier location data must be scoped to active deliveries only"],"low_confidence_areas":["Legal/regulatory requirements for courier GPS data sharing have not been provided by anyone in this scenario","Whether the organization has an existing privacy policy covering employee/contractor location data","Driver retention data — Operations claims drivers are leaving but no quantitative evidence is provided","Customer demand data — Customer Success claims transparency is a differentiator but no customer research is cited"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith based on their domain perspective","The business operates in jurisdictions where GPS tracking of workers may be regulated","The platform has the technical capability to collect courier GPS during deliveries","Industry norms (approximate tracking + ETA) are acceptable to most customers"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact GPS coordinates to customers as a non-negotiable transparency feature. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as a non-negotiable driver-retention measure. These two requirements are mutually exclusive and both are presented as non-negotiable. This MUST be resolved by product leadership before implementation begins.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success team","Operations team","Product Leadership","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A) Exact GPS (Customer Success wins) — show real-time coordinates. Risk: accelerated driver churn, potential regulatory exposure.","B) No visibility at all (Operations wins) — show nothing. Risk: below industry standard, increased support costs, competitive disadvantage.","C) Approximate tracking + ETA (compromise) — show neighborhood-level zone and estimated arrival. Industry standard approach used by Uber, DoorDash, Amazon. Satisfies customer transparency need without exact surveillance. Configurable granularity allows future adjustment.","D) Courier-controlled visibility — let each courier opt in/out of approximate sharing. Respects driver autonomy but creates inconsistent customer experience."],"recommendation":"Option C — Approximate tracking with ETA. This is the proven industry standard that resolves both concerns: customers get meaningful delivery visibility (the actual business need behind Customer Success's request), while couriers are not subjected to exact-coordinate surveillance (the actual harm behind Operations' concern). Neither team's literal requirement is met, but both teams' underlying business objectives are satisfied. Implement with a configurable granularity policy so the level can be tuned by product leadership without code changes."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Implementing exact GPS coordinate sharing to customers as requested by Customer Success","business_rule_broken":"BR-4 — Courier privacy policy must be enforceable. Sharing exact coordinates with no privacy filtering violates privacy-by-design and creates driver retention and regulatory risk."},{"violation":"Implementing zero location visibility as requested by Operations","business_rule_broken":"BR-2 — Customers must receive some form of delivery progress during active delivery. Providing nothing puts the product below industry baseline and harms customer experience."}]}
